Crucible v1.2 and min-mozhi progress posterTypescript

Open Source

React

Angular

React Native

4 min read
5 hours ago

--

When I shipped Crucible v1.0 in March 2026, the elevator pitch was simple: a code generation engine that scaffolds production-ready components into your project — and then gets completely out of the way.
No runtime package. No node_modules black box. No component library you can’t modify.
friend link: click here
Run crucible add Button, and you get a Button.tsx, a Button.module.css, and a Button/README.md—files you own, read, and edit like any other source code.
v1.1 (June 2026) expanded the component library to 25 production-ready components and introduced a plugin architecture so teams can distribute their own components without modifying the engine.
Today I’m releasing Crucible v1.2, and it’s the biggest developer-experience release so far.

The component lifecycle problem
Until v1.2, Crucible solved only half of the workflow: generation.
You could scaffold components and protect edits with content hashes, but the CLI couldn’t answer questions like:
• Has this component drifted from its template?
• What would regeneration actually change?
• How do I safely remove a generated component?
v1.2 completes that workflow with five new lifecycle commands built on the same generation engine.

What’s new
crucible ui — Interactive Terminal Console
The headline feature is an interactive terminal interface.
Running crucible ui (or its aliases wizard and tui) launches a menu-driven console where you can:
• Browse every available component
• Inspect metadata
• Run guided installs
• Execute diff, status, update, and remove commands
…all without memorizing CLI flags.
It’s built entirely on @inquirer/prompts with no additional runtime dependencies.
Running crucible by itself still prints the standard help output—the UI launches only when explicitly requested.

Component lifecycle commands
Crucible now supports the complete lifecycle of generated components.
• crucible info <component> — Displays variants, props, dependencies, and peer dependencies.
• crucible status — Reports component drift (ok / modified / missing), detects stale configuration, supports --json, and exits non-zero for CI.
• crucible diff [component...] — Shows exactly what regeneration would change before writing any files.
• crucible update [component...] — Regenerates tracked components while preserving your edits unless --force is used.
• crucible remove <component...> — Deletes generated files, removes tracking metadata, and warns about dependency relationships.
The status command is one feature I've wanted since v1.0. It can now be integrated directly into CI pipelines with crucible status --json.

React Native (Beta)
Crucible now generates React Native components.
The initial release supports both:
• NativeWind
• StyleSheet
Five components are available today:
• Button
• Input
• Card
• Alert
• Badge
The same component manifest powers both web and mobile generation.

CLI improvements
This release also includes a long list of quality-of-life improvements:
• Shell tab completion
• Update notifications
• — strict plugin mode
• JSON output for list and doctor
• Automatic package-manager detection (npm, pnpm, Yarn, Bun) for peer dependency installation

Security hardening
Several CodeQL findings were addressed in v1.2:
• Prototype-pollution protection during token merging
• Stronger path traversal validation
• Template path validation to prevent code injection
• Removal of Math.random() from generated component IDs

Where Crucible stands today
• 25 production-ready components
• React, Vue 3, Angular, and React Native (Beta)
• CSS Modules, SCSS Modules, Tailwind v4, NativeWind, and StyleSheet
• 556 unit tests
• 245 E2E phases
• Zero runtime footprint

What’s next: v1.3 Migration Engine
One challenge with generated code is the “write once” problem.
Once you’ve customized a generated component, updating it safely becomes difficult.
v1.3 aims to solve this with a true three-way merge engine.
The upcoming crucible upgrade command will use:
• the original generated file,
• your modified version,
• and the latest template,
to apply template improvements while preserving your edits.
The preview capability already exists in crucible diff; the merge engine is the next major milestone.

Something else I’ve been building
Between Crucible releases I’ve also been building Min-Mozhi (மின்மொழி), a modern hardware description language written entirely in Rust.
It compiles to synthesizable Verilog, includes its own event-driven simulator, and is the first Tamil-rooted HDL.
The language uses Go/TypeScript-inspired syntax while providing Rust-like compile-time safety, and supports writing the same hardware in English, Tanglish, or Tamil.
Version 0.1.0 shipped in June with:
• Full compiler pipeline
• Built-in simulator
• Compile-time safety diagnostics
• VS Code extension + LSP
• Hundreds of passing tests
Current development is focused on:
• Clock-domain crossing primitives
• Hardware emulation with real peripherals
• Verilog FFI (extern module)
• Array types and foreach
• Tagged union construction
I’ll share a dedicated article on Min-Mozhi soon — it deserves its own deep dive.
